texts.title: Emerald Tablet (Tabula Smaragdina)
texts.description: The seminal theoretical document of Western and Islamicate alchemical traditions. A compact, cryptic text attributed to Hermes Trismegistus, encoding a synthesis of Aristotelian natural physics and Neoplatonic emanationist cosmology. Earliest known context is within the Arabic Kitab Sirr al-Khaliqa.
texts.transmission_notes: Entered Latin Europe through Arabic science via multiple 12th-century translation vectors. Circulated both embedded in larger works and as a standalone text.

texts.title: Book of the Secret of Creation (Kitab Sirr al-Khaliqa)
texts.description: Expansive cosmological encyclopedia attributed to Balinas (Apollonius of Tyana). Contains the earliest known contextual embedding of the Emerald Tablet. Structured into six books following creation sequence: God, celestial spheres, minerals, plants, animals, humans. Two recensions: A (shorter, closer to Greek original) and B (expanded).
texts.transmission_notes: Also known as Kitab Balaniyus al-Hakim fi'l-Ilal (Book of Wise Balinas on the Causes). Over 30 manuscripts across major libraries. Weisser (1979) critical edition based on 17 manuscripts.

texts.title: Secret of Secrets (Secretum Secretorum)
texts.description: Pseudo-Aristotelian compendium framed as letters from Aristotle to Alexander the Great on statecraft, morality, physiognomy, medicine, astrology, and alchemy. Contains the Emerald Tablet as integrated alchemical and political wisdom. One of the most widely read books of the European High Middle Ages.
texts.transmission_notes: First translated to Latin by John of Seville c. 1140 (abridged), then fully by Philip of Tripoli c. 1243.

texts.title: Second Book of the Elements of Foundation
texts.description: Alchemical treatise attributed to Jabir ibn Hayyan containing an early Arabic version of the Emerald Tablet. Found by Eric Holmyard. Part of the massive Jabirian corpus, likely a collective pseudepigraphical effort by scholars of possible Isma'ili persuasion.
texts.transmission_notes: Part of the Jabirian corpus. Holmyard was the first to find this version of the Emerald Tablet.

texts.title: Book of the Silvery Water and the Starry Earth
texts.description: Alchemical work by Ibn Umayl containing a distinct version of the Emerald Tablet discovery narrative. In this version, an alchemical stone table rests across the knees of Hermes's corpse inside an Egyptian pyramid, inscribed with hieroglyphic writing described as birbawi.
texts.transmission_notes: Provides a unique variant of the discovery narrative linking the Tablet to Egyptian pyramid traditions.

texts.title: On the Secrets of Nature (De secretis naturae)
texts.description: First known Latin translation of the complete Kitab Sirr al-Khaliqa by Hugo of Santalla. Preserves the cosmological context and frame narrative of the Arabic original. Did not achieve widespread circulation compared to later recensions.
texts.transmission_notes: Hugo's version uses 'Superiora de inferioribus, inferiora de superioribus' rather than the vulgate's 'Quod est inferius est sicut quod est superius'.

texts.title: On the Nature of Man (De Natura Hominis)
texts.description: Treatise by Nemesius of Emesa defining the human being as a microcosm binding celestial and terrestrial spheres. One of the first holistic anthropological treatises, heavily engaged with Middle Platonism and Stoicism. The concept of macrocosm-microcosm correspondence is the direct theoretical precursor to the Emerald Tablet's most famous axiom.
texts.transmission_notes: An Arabic translation appears at the end of the Sirr al-Khaliqa, proving the compilers were actively synthesizing Nemesius's Neoplatonic anthropology with alchemical theory.

texts.title: Book of Treasures
texts.description: Syriac compilation by Job of Odessa (Ayyub al-Ruhawi) showing conceptual similarities with the Arabic Sirr al-Khaliqa. Part of the evidence for Syriac intermediary traditions in the Emerald Tablet's transmission.
texts.transmission_notes: Suggests a possible Syriac source for the Sirr al-Khaliqa, though none of the Syriac writings contain the Tablet itself.

texts.title: Vulgate Latin Emerald Tablet
texts.description: The most influential Latin version of the Emerald Tablet, likely translated by Plato of Tivoli in Barcelona. Strips away the narrative framing of the Sirr al-Khaliqa, leaving potent aphorisms. Foundational for commentaries by Albertus Magnus and Roger Bacon.
texts.transmission_notes: Served as the base text for virtually all subsequent European engagement with the Tablet. Preserved in manuscripts like Arundel MS 164.

texts.title: Hortulanus Commentary (Liber super textum Hermetis)
texts.description: The most authoritative medieval exegesis of the Emerald Tablet. Hortulanus interprets the verses as a sequential laboratory manual for creating the Philosopher's Stone, decoding sun/moon imagery through sulfur-mercury theory, and reading the 'separation' as mundification via gentle heat.
texts.transmission_notes: Most widely disseminated commentary. Anchored the Tablet within practical laboratory chemistry.

texts.title: Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae
texts.description: Illustrated alchemical and theosophical work by Heinrich Khunrath. Features elaborate engravings linking the Emerald Tablet to VITRIOL acrostic and deep spiritual introspection.
texts.transmission_notes: First published 1595, expanded edition 1609. Cemented the Tablet's status as the ultimate mystical and chemical icon.

texts.title: Atalanta Fugiens
texts.description: Emblem book by Michael Maier. Emblem I depicts 'the wind carried it in its womb' from the Emerald Tablet. Combines music, image, and text to encode alchemical philosophy.
texts.transmission_notes: Published 1618 in Oppenheim. Part of the broader Renaissance emblematic tradition engaging with Hermetic philosophy.

persons.name: Hermes Trismegistus
persons.description: Syncretic Hellenistic figure combining Greek Hermes and Egyptian Thoth. Traditional author of the Emerald Tablet. In the Sirr al-Khaliqa frame narrative, his tomb contains the tablet inscribed with primordial secrets of nature.

persons.name: Balinas (Pseudo-Apollonius of Tyana)
persons.description: Arabic rendering of the neo-Pythagorean philosopher Apollonius of Tyana. In Arabic sources, revered as 'Lord of the Talismans' (sahib al-tilasmat). Attributed discoverer/transmitter of the Emerald Tablet in the Sirr al-Khaliqa frame narrative.

persons.name: Jabir ibn Hayyan
persons.description: Foundational figure of Islamic alchemy. The massive Jabirian corpus (hundreds of treatises) was likely a collective pseudepigraphical effort, possibly of Isma'ili persuasion (Kraus). The Emerald Tablet appears in the Kitab Ustuqus al-Uss attributed to him.

persons.name: Ibn Umayl
persons.description: Tenth-century alchemist who preserved a distinct version of the Emerald Tablet discovery narrative in the Book of the Silvery Water and the Starry Earth, placing the tablet in an Egyptian pyramid.

persons.name: Nemesius of Emesa
persons.description: Christian bishop who composed De Natura Hominis, defining the human being as a microcosm. His concept of macrocosm-microcosm correspondence is the theoretical precursor to the Emerald Tablet's central axiom.

persons.name: Job of Odessa
persons.description: Author of the Syriac Book of Treasures, which shows conceptual similarities with the Sirr al-Khaliqa.

persons.name: Sagijus of Nablus
persons.description: Christian priest named in an introductory preface to one of the Arabic recensions of the Emerald Tablet. May represent a historical memory of the 6th-century Syriac translator Sergius of Reshaina.

persons.name: Hugo of Santalla
persons.description: Spanish translator who produced the first known Latin translation of the complete Sirr al-Khaliqa as De secretis naturae (c. 1145-1151). His version preserves the Arabic frame narrative but did not circulate widely.

persons.name: John of Seville
persons.description: Translated an abridged version of the Secretum Secretorum into Latin c. 1140, focusing on medical and astrological content.

persons.name: Philip of Tripoli
persons.description: Completed a full Latin translation of the Secretum Secretorum c. 1243. Because of Aristotle's unassailable authority, this became one of the most widely read books of the High Middle Ages.

persons.name: Plato of Tivoli
persons.description: Italian mathematician and astronomer who likely produced the Vulgate Latin Emerald Tablet (c. 1134-1145) in Barcelona. Attribution conjectural but widely accepted.

persons.name: Albertus Magnus
persons.description: Dominican friar and scholastic philosopher who cited the Emerald Tablet in his Book of Minerals, helping integrate it into scholastic natural philosophy.

persons.name: Roger Bacon
persons.description: Franciscan friar who engaged with the Vulgate Emerald Tablet, contributing to its scholarly legitimacy within medieval university culture.

persons.name: Hortulanus
persons.description: Obscure but highly influential alchemist who wrote the most authoritative medieval commentary on the Emerald Tablet (Liber super textum Hermetis), interpreting it as a sequential laboratory manual for creating the Philosopher's Stone.

persons.name: Heinrich Khunrath
persons.description: German physician, alchemist, and Hermetic philosopher. His Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae (1609) features elaborate engravings linking the Emerald Tablet to the VITRIOL acrostic.

persons.name: Michael Maier
persons.description: German physician and alchemist. His Atalanta Fugiens (1618) dedicated its first emblem to the Emerald Tablet phrase 'the wind carried it in its womb'.

persons.name: Isaac Newton
persons.description: Translated the Emerald Tablet into English and composed a detailed commentary (Keynes MS 28). Read 'telesmi' as 'perfection', linking alchemical operations to Biblical Chaos and gravitational forces.

persons.name: Julius Ruska
persons.description: Foundational modern philologist of the Emerald Tablet. Published Tabula Smaragdina (1926), establishing the Arabic and Latin textual history. Dated composition between 600-750 CE.

persons.name: Eric John Holmyard
persons.description: First to find an early Arabic version of the Emerald Tablet in the Jabirian Kitab Ustuqus al-Uss al-Thani.

persons.name: Paul Kraus
persons.description: Demonstrated that the Jabirian corpus was likely a collective pseudepigraphical effort. Analyzed the Sirr al-Khaliqa as providing theoretical foundation for alchemical operations encoded in the Emerald Tablet.

persons.name: Ursula Weisser
persons.description: Produced the critical edition of the Sirr al-Khaliqa (1979, Aleppo), analyzing over 20 manuscripts and establishing two recensions (A and B). Originating as Frankfurt dissertation (1974) under Fuat Sezgin.

persons.name: Silvestre de Sacy
persons.description: French orientalist who in 1799 demonstrated the connection between the Sirr al-Khaliqa and Apollonius of Tyana, correcting the earlier misidentification as an Arabic extract of Pliny.

translations.title: Arabic (Sirr al-Khaliqa tradition)
translations.notes: Standard Arabic form associated with the Balinas tradition. Minor variants across manuscripts.

translations.title: Latin Vulgate
translations.notes: The dominant European version. Strips narrative framing, leaves stark aphorisms. Attribution to Plato of Tivoli is conjectural.

translations.title: Hugo of Santalla Latin
translations.notes: Begins 'Superiora de inferioribus, inferiora de superioribus'. Preserves frame narrative. Did not circulate widely.

translations.title: Newton's English Translation
translations.notes: Based on the Latin vulgate. Newton replaced 'telesmi' with 'perfection'.

translations.title: Jabirian Arabic Version
translations.notes: Found by Holmyard in the Kitab Ustuqus al-Uss al-Thani.

translations.title: Anonymous Arabic Version (Ruska)
translations.notes: 12th century recension found by Ruska, claiming to have been dictated by Sagijus of Nablus.

translations.title: Secretum Secretorum Latin
translations.notes: Philip of Tripoli's full translation of the Secretum Secretorum.

translations.title: Blavatsky's Translation
translations.source_citation: H. P. Blavatsky, Isis Unveiled (1877) or Secret Doctrine (1888)

concepts.label: Macrocosm and Microcosm
concepts.definition_short: The principle that the structure of the human being (microcosm) mirrors the structure of the universe (macrocosm).
concepts.definition_long: The foundational axiom of Hermetic philosophy, formalized in the Emerald Tablet as 'That which is above is like to that which is below.' Unlike Gnostic systems that viewed the material world as flawed, Hermetic philosophy embraces the visible world as a divine reflection of the creator. Operations on the microcosmic scale (laboratory alchemy) participate in macrocosmic divine creation.
concepts.significance: The Tablet's most famous and enduring axiom. Derives from Nemesius of Emesa's anthropology via late antique Neoplatonism.

concepts.label: Four Elemental Qualities
concepts.definition_short: The four primary qualities (hot, cold, moist, dry) that combine to form the four elements and drive all material transformations.
concepts.definition_long: The Sirr al-Khaliqa grounds all natural phenomena in the theory of four primary qualities emerging from primordial homogeneous matter. Combinations produce four elements (fire, air, water, earth). All properties of substances and processes are derived deductively through interactions and proportions of these qualities. This is the Aristotelian substrate of the Emerald Tablet's alchemical operations.
concepts.significance: Provides the theoretical framework within which the Tablet's instructions for separation and recombination become intelligible.

concepts.label: Emanation (Exitus a Deo)
concepts.definition_short: The Neoplatonic doctrine that material multiplicity cascades from a singular, indivisible source.
concepts.definition_long: The Tablet states: 'As all things were from One, by mediation of a single one, so all things were born from this one reality by a single process of adaptation.' This encapsulates the Neoplatonic doctrine of emanation. A 'One Mind' (Nous) mediates, binding celestial and terrestrial realms. Alchemical practice reverses this emanation: the substance ascends from earth to heaven (sublimation) then descends (fixation), uniting superior and inferior forces.
concepts.significance: Overlays the Aristotelian material framework with a cosmological structure that makes alchemy a spiritual practice, not just a physical one.

concepts.label: Telesmi / Telesmum
concepts.definition_short: Transliterated Arabic term (abu al-tilasmat, 'father of talismans') that entered Latin untranslated and became one of the most disputed words in alchemical exegesis.
concepts.definition_long: The Arabic text describes the Tablet's subject as 'abu al-tilasmat' (father of talismans). Early Latin translators like Plato of Tivoli simply transliterated this as 'telesmi', retaining a link to talismanic magic. Newton, lacking Arabic context, replaced it with 'perfection'. The word's opacity in Latin generated centuries of interpretive controversy.
concepts.significance: A key example of how the Latin tradition actively created new interpretive problems through transliteration rather than translation.

concepts.label: Sulfur-Mercury Theory
concepts.definition_short: The doctrine that all metals are composed of philosophical sulfur (active masculine principle) and philosophical mercury (passive feminine principle).
concepts.definition_long: Hortulanus decoded 'The Sun is its father, the Moon its mother' as the chemical wedding of Philosophers' Gold (Sol, active seed) and Philosophers' Silver (Luna, passive receptacle). 'The wind carried it in its womb' refers to conception within the sealed retort's vaporous atmosphere. 'The earth is its nurse' dictates recombination with the solid ferment for stability.
concepts.significance: The dominant interpretive lens through which the Emerald Tablet was read as practical laboratory instruction from the 14th century onward.

concepts.label: Decknamen (Cover Names)
concepts.definition_short: The systematic use of symbolic cover names in alchemical texts, where a single term simultaneously refers to celestial bodies, metals, and operative stages.
concepts.definition_long: Alchemical texts use Decknamen such that 'Sun' may simultaneously refer to the celestial body, the metal gold, or a specific operative stage. Standard text analysis methods fail to capture this semantic density. Digital analysis requires domain-specific ontologies (RDF/SKOS) to disambiguate contextual semantic roles.
concepts.significance: Understanding Decknamen is essential for interpreting the Emerald Tablet and all alchemical literature. The term 'telesmi' is a special case where even the cover-name mechanism broke down in translation.

concepts.label: VITRIOL Acrostic
concepts.definition_short: The Latin acrostic 'Visita Interiora Terrae Rectificando Invenies Occultum Lapidem' (Visit the interior of the earth and by rectifying you will find the hidden stone).
concepts.definition_long: Consistently paired with the Emerald Tablet in early modern print culture, linking the text to both the production of sulfuric acid and deep spiritual introspection. Featured prominently in Khunrath's Amphitheatrum (1609).
concepts.significance: Represents the early modern fusion of practical chemistry and spiritual allegory in the reception of the Emerald Tablet.

concepts.label: Discovery Topos (Hidden Chamber Narrative)
concepts.definition_short: The late antique literary motif of discovering ancient wisdom in a hidden underground chamber, used to authenticate esoteric knowledge.
concepts.definition_long: The Emerald Tablet is introduced through a pseudo-revelatory account in which Balinas discovers a hidden chamber containing both a book and a tablet attributed to Hermes. This combines two motifs: finding a buried text and receiving a celestial inscription. The topos functions as a legitimizing device for the text's authority.
concepts.significance: The frame narrative is essential for understanding how the Tablet was received: not as a free-floating oracle but as a discovered artifact with claimed ancient provenance.

bibliography.author: Ursula Weisser
bibliography.title: Buch über das Geheimnis der Schöpfung und die Darstellung der Natur (Buch der Ursachen) von Pseudo-Apollonios von Tyana
bibliography.publisher: Institute for the History of Arabic Science, University of Aleppo
bibliography.notes: Critical edition of Arabic Sirr al-Khaliqa. Based on collation of 17 manuscripts. Establishes Recension A and B.

bibliography.author: Robert Steele and Dorothea Waley Singer
bibliography.title: The Emerald Table
bibliography.journal: Proceedings of the Royal Society of Medicine
bibliography.notes: Classic study and edition of the Latin tradition. Source of the well-known vulgate text.

bibliography.author: Françoise Hudry
bibliography.title: Le De secretis nature du ps.-Apollonius de Tyane, traduction latine par Hugues de Santalla du Kitâb sirr al-halîqa
bibliography.notes: Key modern edition for Hugo of Santalla's Latin translation. Chrysopoeia 6 (1997-1999): 1-154.

bibliography.author: Didier Kahn
bibliography.title: La table d'émeraude et sa tradition alchimique
bibliography.notes: Central modern monograph on the Tablet's textual and interpretive tradition.

bibliography.author: Julius Ruska
bibliography.title: Tabula Smaragdina: Ein Beitrag zur Geschichte der hermetischen Literatur
bibliography.notes: Foundational modern philological study. Dated composition 600-750 CE.

bibliography.author: William R. Newman
bibliography.title: Newton the Alchemist: Science, Enigma, and the Quest for Nature's Secret Fire
bibliography.notes: Useful for early modern and Newtonian reception of the Emerald Tablet.

bibliography.author: M. David Litwa
bibliography.title: Hermetica II: The Excerpts of Stobaeus, Papyrus Fragments, and Ancient Testimonies
bibliography.notes: Modern synthesis with Emerald Tablet chapter, notes, and bibliography.

bibliography.author: Eric John Holmyard
bibliography.title: Alchemy
bibliography.notes: Standard history of alchemy. Holmyard's account of finding the Jabirian version.

bibliography.author: Eric John Holmyard
bibliography.title: The Emerald Table
bibliography.notes: Early scholarly discussion of the Emerald Tablet's Arabic context.

bibliography.author: Paul Kraus
bibliography.title: Jābir ibn Ḥayyān: Contribution à l'histoire des idées scientifiques dans l'Islam
bibliography.notes: Demonstrated collective authorship of Jabirian corpus. Analyzed Sirr al-Khaliqa sources and influence.

bibliography.author: Joseph Needham
bibliography.title: Science and Civilisation in China
bibliography.notes: Suggested possible Chinese alchemical origins for the Emerald Tablet. Minority position.

bibliography.author: Silvestre de Sacy
bibliography.title: Le livre du secret de la créature
bibliography.notes: Demonstrated connection between Sirr al-Khaliqa and Apollonius of Tyana, correcting Pliny misidentification.

bibliography.author: Bauer and Lindemann (TEAM LAXiTY)
bibliography.title: History and Translations of Emerald Tablet
bibliography.notes: Collected translations with verse numbering for comparison. Our primary source for parallel translation data.

bibliography.author: Sarah A. Lang
bibliography.title: A Machine Reasoning Algorithm for the Digital Analysis of Alchemical Language and its Decknamen
bibliography.notes: Methodology for digital analysis of alchemical polyvalent terminology.

bibliography.author: Betty Jo Teeter Dobbs
bibliography.title: The Foundations of Newton's Alchemy
bibliography.notes: Key work on Newton's alchemical studies.

manuscripts.contents_summary: Major manuscript of the Sirr al-Khaliqa containing the Emerald Tablet in its original cosmological context.
manuscripts.significance: One of the key Arabic manuscript witnesses used by Weisser in her critical edition.

manuscripts.contents_summary: Alchemical manuscript collection compiled by Augustinian friars in Nuremberg containing the Vulgate Latin Emerald Tablet.
manuscripts.significance: Preserves the Vulgate Latin variant used by Albertus Magnus and Roger Bacon.

manuscripts.contents_summary: Isaac Newton's English translation and detailed commentary on the Emerald Tablet.
manuscripts.significance: Demonstrates continuity of Hermetic tradition into early modern natural philosophy. Newton links alchemy to Biblical Chaos and gravitational forces.

manuscripts.repository: Sächsische Landesbibliothek - Staats- und Universitätsbibliothek Dresden
manuscripts.contents_summary: Early modern Latin printed edition of Hermetic texts associated with Hermes Trismegistus.
manuscripts.significance: Represents the early modern print culture engagement with Hermetic philosophy.

timeline_events.title: Nemesius of Emesa writes De Natura Hominis
timeline_events.description: Defines the human being as a microcosm binding celestial and terrestrial spheres. Direct theoretical precursor to the Emerald Tablet's central axiom.

timeline_events.title: Sirr al-Khaliqa composed/compiled
timeline_events.description: The cosmological encyclopedia attributed to Balinas is written, containing the earliest known contextual embedding of the Emerald Tablet.

timeline_events.title: Secretum Secretorum compiled
timeline_events.description: Pseudo-Aristotelian compendium compiled, embedding the Emerald Tablet within political-alchemical wisdom addressed to Alexander the Great.

timeline_events.title: Jabirian Emerald Tablet version produced
timeline_events.description: The Emerald Tablet appears in the Kitab Ustuqus al-Uss al-Thani, attributed to Jabir ibn Hayyan.

timeline_events.title: Ibn Umayl's Emerald Tablet version
timeline_events.description: Ibn Umayl preserves a distinct discovery narrative placing the Tablet in an Egyptian pyramid.

timeline_events.title: John of Seville translates abridged Secretum Secretorum
timeline_events.description: First Latin translation of the pseudo-Aristotelian Secretum Secretorum, focused on medical and astrological content.

timeline_events.title: Vulgate Latin Emerald Tablet produced
timeline_events.description: Plato of Tivoli (probably) produces the standalone Latin Emerald Tablet in Barcelona. This becomes the dominant European version.

timeline_events.title: Hugo of Santalla translates Sirr al-Khaliqa
timeline_events.description: First complete Latin translation of the Kitab Sirr al-Khaliqa as De secretis naturae. Preserves Arabic frame narrative but does not circulate widely.

timeline_events.title: Philip of Tripoli translates full Secretum Secretorum
timeline_events.description: Complete Latin translation becomes one of the most widely read books of the High Middle Ages due to Aristotle's authority.

timeline_events.title: Hortulanus writes commentary on Emerald Tablet
timeline_events.description: The most authoritative medieval exegesis, interpreting the Tablet as a laboratory manual for the Philosopher's Stone.

timeline_events.title: Khunrath publishes Amphitheatrum Sapientiae Aeternae
timeline_events.description: Expanded edition with elaborate engravings linking the Emerald Tablet to VITRIOL acrostic and spiritual introspection.

timeline_events.title: Michael Maier publishes Atalanta Fugiens
timeline_events.description: Emblem I depicts 'the wind carried it in its womb' from the Emerald Tablet.

timeline_events.title: Kriegsmann publishes Phoenician rendering
timeline_events.description: Speculative rendering of the Emerald Tablet in Phoenician.

timeline_events.title: Isaac Newton translates and comments on Emerald Tablet
timeline_events.description: Newton painstakingly translates the Tablet and composes a detailed commentary (Keynes MS 28), linking alchemical operations to Biblical Chaos and gravitational forces.

timeline_events.title: Silvestre de Sacy identifies Apollonius connection
timeline_events.description: Demonstrates the connection between the Sirr al-Khaliqa and Apollonius of Tyana, correcting earlier misidentification as an Arabic extract of Pliny.

timeline_events.title: Holmyard finds Jabirian version
timeline_events.description: Eric Holmyard discovers an early Arabic version of the Emerald Tablet in the Jabirian corpus.

timeline_events.title: Ruska publishes Tabula Smaragdina
timeline_events.description: Foundational modern philological study establishing the Arabic and Latin textual history of the Emerald Tablet.

timeline_events.title: Kraus analyzes Jabirian corpus and Sirr al-Khaliqa
timeline_events.description: Demonstrates collective authorship of Jabirian corpus. Identifies parallels with Nemesius and Syriac Book of Treasures.

timeline_events.title: Weisser publishes critical edition of Sirr al-Khaliqa
timeline_events.description: Aleppo edition based on 17 manuscripts, establishing Recension A and B. Demonstrates the Tablet's original cosmological context.

text_relationships.notes: The Tablet appears at the conclusion of the cosmological encyclopedia.
The Tablet is embedded as integrated alchemical-political wisdom.
Jabirian version of the Tablet found by Holmyard.
Ibn Umayl's distinct discovery narrative variant.
Hugo of Santalla's Latin translation of the complete work.
Line-by-line exegesis interpreting as laboratory manual.
The dominant Latin recension, stripped of frame narrative.
Arabic translation of Nemesius appears at end of Sirr al-Khaliqa.
